picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom secured a $30B multi-year chip deal with Apple (confirmed July 8) while simultaneously building out its XPU/ASIC business for hyperscalers at >150% YoY AI revenue growth — two durable, contracted revenue streams that underpin upward FY2026/FY2027 estimate revisions. The stock defied Wednesday's tech selloff (+3.3% on Jul 9 vs. XLK -2.57% the prior day) on the Apple deal headline, validating relative resilience. Appaloosa (Tepper) and Tiger Global both hold/added AVGO in Q1 2026 13F filings, while the stock trades at ~61x trailing P/E with FCF yield improving toward 5%+ as VMware cross-sell accelerates — FCF quality separates it from pure-play semis.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (~early September 2026) with updated AI XPU revenue guidance and Apple chip program ramp details
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$403, roughly 18% below its 52-week high of $495, consolidating above key support after the Apple deal re-rate; FCF yield approaching 5% provides fundamental floor vs. peers at zero yield
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 60% on VMware integration drag, or loss of a top-2 hyperscaler XPU design win to a competitor
picks.keyRisk: Erste Group downgraded AVGO to Hold on valuation concerns July 7 — if AI capex cycle pauses, the 60x+ multiple compresses rapidly

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron gained 4.5% today (Jul 9) on a domestic investment announcement that gave investors a fresh catalyst beyond oversold bounce dynamics, and UBS called Micron '25% cheaper' with chip stocks 'far from bubble territory.' HBM memory is supply-constrained through 2026 with long-term contracts locking in pricing, delivering A+ grades on growth, profitability, and momentum per Seeking Alpha's quant model. The stock's 746% TTM run is fundamental-driven — FY estimates have been revised sharply higher as HBM ASPs surge — with valuation having improved from B to A- even after the enormous run.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings report (late September 2026) — HBM revenue mix and pricing guidance for FY2027
picks.entryNote: Pulled back from highs but recovered strongly today; UBS note frames the current level as an attractive re-entry versus fundamental earnings power; trading well below recent peak with momentum re-establishing
picks.exitTrigger: HBM pricing contracts show rollover below cost-plus margin floor, or Samsung/SK Hynix supply floods the market faster than anticipated
picks.keyRisk: SK Hynix U.S. IPO (pricing this week) brings a direct HBM competitor to domestic investors, potentially compressing MU's valuation premium

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 agentic model was released today with Zuckerberg citing 'strongest model for agentic and coding work yet,' reinforcing the company's pivot from pure advertiser to AI model provider and cloud compute seller — a structural re-rating catalyst. Ackman's Pershing Square added META and holds it as a core position; the stock's advertising flywheel continues generating >$50B+ in annual FCF while the cloud compute buildout optionality is not yet in consensus numbers. The stock is down modestly today (-0.36%) despite the model launch, offering a favorable entry vs. the fundamental step-up.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first report to include cloud compute revenue line-item and AI monetization metrics from Muse models
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$601, consolidating after the July 1 +9% gap on cloud infrastructure news; the thesis has expanded from ads-only to ads + cloud + AI models, but the stock has not yet fully re-rated for the incremental TAM
picks.exitTrigger: Cloud compute buildout shows customer adoption below 3 hyperscaler-equivalent deals by year-end, or core advertising revenue growth decelerates below 15% YoY
picks.keyRisk: Heavy capex cycle (>$60B projected for 2026) could pressure FCF if AI cloud monetization takes longer than 12 months to materialize

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Berkshire Hathaway (Greg Abel) made its most decisive Q1 2026 bet with a +224% increase to ~$16.6B in GOOGL — the loudest single institutional signal of the 13F cycle — citing AI infrastructure moat at a reasonable multiple vs. peers. Despite falling 2.5% today as the 'largest drag' on the index amid hardware rotation, this reprices GOOGL more attractively as Search monetization, YouTube, and Google Cloud AI all continue to scale. The Q2 earnings report in late July is the forcing function for re-rating.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Google Cloud AI revenue growth rate and Search AI Overviews monetization disclosure
picks.entryNote: Pulled back today to more attractive levels; Berkshire's massive add signals valuation comfort at these levels; trading at a meaningful discount to MSFT on forward P/E despite comparable AI infrastructure advantages
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud market share loss to AWS/Azure accelerates, or Search query volume declines meaningfully from AI chatbot substitution documented in user data
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedy in the Search monopoly case could force structural remedies (Chrome/Android divestiture) that permanently impair the advertising moat

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: XLF gained 2.18% on July 8 following clean Fed stress test results and bank buyback/dividend hike announcements, with the Financial Select Sector SPDR hitting an all-time high — Wells Fargo analyst Mike Mayo called this 'one of the best environments I've seen in three decades.' Goldman specifically benefits from the M&A revival (healthcare mega-deals, AI company IPOs), record capital markets activity, and higher-for-longer rates supporting net interest margins. Q2 2026 earnings on July 14 provide the next hard catalyst with major bank results alongside Warsh's Congressional testimony.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings on July 14 — investment banking revenue, FICC trading, and capital return guidance
picks.entryNote: Financials sector hit all-time intraday high this week with XLF RSI at 77 (overbought near-term), suggesting a brief consolidation then continuation; GS trades at ~13x forward earnings vs. 10-year median ~11x — modest premium justified by capital markets cycle upgrade
picks.exitTrigger: Q2 IB revenue misses consensus by >15% or management signals M&A pipeline deterioration; Fed pivots to cutting rates sharply (compresses NIM)
picks.keyRisk: Rapid rise in private credit markets creates competitive disintermediation of Goldman's lending and structured products business over 2-3 year horizon

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the cleanest capital-light compounder in financials — ratings revenue benefits directly from the record corporate debt issuance cycle (Amazon just announced a $25B bond raise), while the Market Intelligence and Platts divisions provide durable subscription revenue growing at high-single-digits. The stock has appeared consistently in the prior week's positioning as a quality anchor. SPGI screens across multiple tracked institutional fund lists as a 'quality compounder at reasonable multiple' — the hallmark of a name where smart money parks capital in late-cycle with confidence.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — ratings issuance volume guidance and Market Intelligence ARR growth versus consensus
picks.entryNote: Trading near 30x forward earnings vs. 5-year average of 33x — compressed valuation relative to history given rate headwinds that are now fading as issuance volumes recover; record corporate bond issuance in 2026 is a direct revenue tailwind
picks.exitTrigger: Corporate bond issuance collapses due to credit spread widening >200bps IG, or antitrust action forces Dow Jones Indices divestiture
picks.keyRisk: Cyclical ratings revenue exposed to credit market freeze if rates spike sharply on hot CPI print July 14

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: Active U.S.-Iran strikes (the U.S. launched new airstrikes against Iran Thursday morning with Tehran responding) directly validate the defense spending thesis and RTX's missile/air defense backlog durability. The late-cycle macro regime with elevated geopolitical risk creates sustained demand for Raytheon's Patriot, AMRAAM, and StormBreaker programs. Defense spending is rate-agnostic and politically bipartisan, providing an inflation-hedge quality that most industrials lack. RTX has been a consistent presence in prior week's positioning with the geopolitical risk vector now confirmed live.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Raytheon defense backlog update and Pratt & Whitney GTF engine remediation completion timeline
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 21x forward earnings, near the middle of its historical range; Pratt GTF overhaul overhang is well-understood by the market and is resolving — any clarity on remediation completion is incremental positive
picks.exitTrigger: U.S.-Iran ceasefire holds durably for 90+ days reducing defense procurement urgency, or Pratt GTF costs materially exceed the $3B remediation reserve
picks.keyRisk: GTF engine inspection costs re-accelerate in Q2 disclosure, compressing FY2026 EPS below consensus and forcing a guide-down

picks.name: Quanta Services Inc.
picks.rationale: Quanta is the dominant electrical infrastructure contractor with record-high backlog directly tied to AI data center buildout, grid modernization, and renewable interconnection — all three secular tailwinds are intact regardless of near-term macro. FY2026 estimates were revised upward after Q1 beat, and 3M/6M momentum vs. SPY is consistently positive. The industrials sector has been the #1 ranked sector throughout this week's rotation analysis, and PWR represents the cleanest pick-and-shovel play at the intersection of AI power demand and grid infrastructure.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — backlog growth, revenue guidance raise, and data center contract announcements
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 35x forward earnings, reflecting record backlog premium; technically consolidating near all-time highs after industrials sector reached multi-year relative highs vs. S&P 500 in the current week
picks.exitTrigger: Backlog growth decelerates below 10% YoY or margins compress below 9% EBIT as labor/materials inflation exceeds project pricing
picks.keyRisk: Hyperscaler capex pause (Tail Risk #1) directly reduces data center grid interconnection project pipeline — single biggest source of incremental backlog growth

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: Vistra is the largest competitive power generator in the U.S. with natural gas and nuclear capacity — natural gas is confirmed as the dominant near-term power source for AI data centers, and Brent crude's retreat to $72 on Iran ceasefire hopes briefly lowered input costs before reversing. Tepper (Appaloosa) doubled his VST position in Q1 2026 13F, the strongest institutional validation in the power sector. The energy sector (XLE) is confirmed Tier 1 with +22% YTD, and Vistra's nuclear/gas combination gives it non-intermittent power supply premium that pure renewables cannot match.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — power price realizations, capacity contract expansion announcements, nuclear PPA deal with data center customers
picks.entryNote: FCF yield >4%, ROIC expanding, 4/4 EPS beats historically; energy sector confirmed at all-time highs with geopolitical premium partially reversed today as Iran situation shows ceasefire optionality — potentially creating a re-entry window at lower energy input costs
picks.exitTrigger: Power market deregulation in Texas (ERCOT) forces capacity price reset below Vistra's assumed $45+/kWh contract pricing, or nuclear plant safety incident triggers regulatory shutdown
picks.keyRisk: Oil/gas price collapse (Iran deal) reduces the power price premium in competitive markets, compressing Vistra's merchant power margins

picks.name: CrowdStrike Holdings Inc.
picks.rationale: CrowdStrike appeared in prior-week positioning (Jul 7-8) as the preferred cybersecurity name, with the recovery from the July 2024 outage now complete — ARR re-acceleration is confirmed and the platform consolidation thesis (enterprises reducing vendor count) is playing out in enterprise win rates. With AI-native threats proliferating in a world of autonomous agents and agentic AI (Meta's Muse Spark 1.1 released today), the security attack surface is expanding structurally. CRWD maintains >30% FCF margin and >76% gross margin, making it the highest-quality FCF generator in enterprise security.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings (late August 2026) — ARR net new additions and GovCloud contract wins from DOGE-related federal IT modernization
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 70x forward earnings — premium, but FCF yield >1.5% and growing rapidly; prior highs recaptured post-incident recovery, consolidating at technical support with sector rotation into tech quality names on July 9 rally
picks.exitTrigger: ARR net new additions miss consensus by >15% for two consecutive quarters, signaling customer churn is higher than the 'incident-resolved' narrative assumes
picks.keyRisk: Microsoft Defender and Palo Alto Networks continue platform consolidation push at lower ASPs, threatening CrowdStrike's mid-market expansion pricing power

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the portfolio's primary macro hedge against the three live tail risks: fiscal dominance (US deficit >$2T/year), inflation re-acceleration (headline CPI 4.17% with 8.2% 3-month annualized), and geopolitical shock (active US-Iran strikes as of today). Real policy rate is barely positive at ~+0.2%, which is historically the sweet spot for gold outperformance. GLD pulled back from January 2026 all-time high above $509 to ~$380, creating a favorable re-entry with institutional flows confirmed via the sector rotation data showing 'real assets leading the charge in 2026.'
picks.catalyst: June CPI print July 14 — a hot print (>4.5% YoY) reanchors gold as the preferred inflation hedge over long-duration Treasuries
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$380, roughly 25% below January 2026 all-time highs — the pullback has absorbed speculative froth while central bank buying flows remain structurally intact; entry at 52-week midpoint with asymmetric upside on any inflation surprise
picks.exitTrigger: Fed hikes rates aggressively (>100bp in 2026) pushing real rates above +1.5%, destroying the gold carry narrative
picks.keyRisk: Dollar strengthening on 'higher for longer' Fed narrative compresses gold's USD-denominated price despite strong physical demand

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides quality factor + income in a single 0.06% expense vehicle, holding high-FCF, low-debt dividend compounders that have low correlation to AI-theme drawdowns by construction. In the current late-cycle regime with a near-zero equity risk premium, SCHD's 3.5% dividend yield provides positive carry while the value rotation confirmed in June (VTV +3.4% when S&P -1%) is structurally supportive. Consumer Staples and Healthcare — SCHD's largest sector exposures — are both confirmed Tier 1 and 2 in the sector rotation rankings.
picks.catalyst: Sustained value-over-growth rotation confirmation (June's VTV outperformance extending into Q3 2026 earnings season)
picks.entryNote: 3.5% dividend yield provides income floor; no single name above 5% weight; structurally underweight mega-cap tech means no AI-drawdown correlation risk; trading at fair value vs. historical
picks.exitTrigger: Dividend coverage ratios at top-10 holdings deteriorate below 1.2x as earnings slow in a hard landing scenario
picks.keyRisk: Sharp equity market-wide selloff compresses even high-quality dividend names as forced selling dominates over fundamentals temporarily

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire's $380-397B cash fortress at 5%+ T-bill yield is effectively a free option on market dislocation — every day that markets don't sell off, Berkshire earns ~$20B/year risk-free while Greg Abel waits for the 'fat pitch.' Beta below 0.7, zero holding-company debt, and diversified operating earnings (BNSF, GEICO, Berkshire Energy) make this the most capital-efficient defensive position in the universe. The Q1 2026 13F renovation (40 → 26 positions, GOOGL +224%) signals Abel's high-conviction deployment appetite when valuation cooperates.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (early August) — cash deployment decision or share repurchase acceleration confirms Abel's capital allocation discipline
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 1.5-1.6x book value — at the low end of the last 5-year range; historically Berkshire has been a strong absolute performer when purchased at book multiples below 1.6x
picks.exitTrigger: Abel deploys >$100B into a single acquisition at a premium multiple, concentrating risk that the diversification model is built to avoid
picks.keyRisk: Berkshire's massive AAPL position (~22% of equity portfolio) creates residual AI-tech sentiment exposure despite the defensive framing

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate-duration Treasury exposure with a current yield near 4.1% — attractive income with less duration risk than TLT in a 'higher for longer' regime. The 10-year Treasury at 4.49% is at the upper end of the 2026 range, meaning any dovish surprise (labor market deterioration accelerating, CPI moderating) creates mark-to-market gain. IEF serves as the portfolio's primary negative-beta hedge in a risk-off scenario — when the AI CapEx Cliff tail risk materializes, Treasuries rally sharply. Medium conviction reflects the real risk of a further rate spike.
picks.catalyst: June CPI print July 14 — a soft print (<3.8% YoY) triggers immediate Treasury rally and IEF appreciation
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.49% — near the top of its recent trading range; entering with yield-to-maturity close to all-in cost provides income cushion even if rates rise modestly; intermediate duration limits blow-up risk vs. TLT
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yields breach 5.25%, signaling the fiscal dominance tail risk is materializing — rotate to TIPS or reduce duration entirely
picks.keyRisk: Hawkish June FOMC minutes (released July 8) with 9 of 18 officials favoring a rate hike — a hot CPI print on July 14 could push 10-year to 4.75%+, creating meaningful mark-to-market loss

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: Consumer Staples hit its 8th record high of 2026 on July 7, confirming a defensive rotation that is multi-week in character — not a single-day blip. KO is the cleanest expression of the defensive consumer staples thesis: global pricing power confirmed by Walmart announcing KO product price cuts (a signal that volumes are strong enough for Walmart to negotiate, not that KO is losing pricing), Berkshire holds KO as a core position, and the dividend yield ~3% provides income support. The stock is a late-cycle safe harbor with 60+ years of consecutive dividend growth.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth guidance and EM market volume trends confirming global demand durability
picks.entryNote: Consumer Staples sector at all-time highs with forward P/E ~19-20x vs. 10-year median of ~17x — modestly rich but the defensive rotation premium is justified given the late-cycle macro regime; Berkshire's long-held position provides institutional anchor
picks.exitTrigger: Organic revenue growth decelerates below 4% YoY for two consecutive quarters, signaling consumer trade-down to private label has structurally impaired pricing power
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 weight-loss drug adoption meaningfully reduces caloric beverage consumption volumes in key markets (US, Europe) — a slow-moving but structural secular headwind

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle regime where AI infrastructure capex remains the dominant earnings engine, inflation is re-accelerating (CPI 4.17% YoY, 8.2% 3-month annualized), the Fed is hawkishly on hold with 9 of 18 members favoring a hike, and geopolitical risk (active US-Iran strikes as of today) keeps energy and defense bid. The growth book concentrates highest conviction in AI infrastructure hardware (AVGO, MU) that demonstrated relative strength today with the semiconductor bounce (+2.5% SMH), balanced against a diversified mix of defensively-valued financials (GS, SPGI), industrial capex beneficiaries (RTX, PWR), power infrastructure (VST), enterprise security (CRWD), and the two Communication Services names (META, GOOGL) that provide AI platform monetization exposure. Carry-overs from the prior 7-day positioning include AVGO (returned to #1 from #2 on the $30B Apple deal), MU (reinstated on today's 4.5% rally and domestic investment catalyst), META, GOOGL, GS, SPGI, RTX, PWR, VST, and CRWD — ISRG was rotated out given today's sector evidence favoring hardware/semis over medtech, replaced by GS which gets a direct earnings catalyst July 14. The dominant risk being underwritten is an AI CapEx Cliff scenario where one or more hyperscalers guides capex flat at Q2 earnings — the defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is sized at defensiveScore 4 to provide meaningful but not capital-crippling protection against this outcome while preserving full exposure to the ongoing AI infrastructure supercycle.
diversificationNote: The growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors: Information Technology (AVGO, MU, CRWD — 3 names, hard limit reached, but serving different points in the AI value chain: custom silicon, memory, and security), Communication Services (META, GOOGL), Financials (GS, SPGI), Industrials (RTX, PWR), and Utilities (VST); AVGO and MU share AI hardware exposure but serve fundamentally different end markets (networking/ASIC vs. HBM memory). The defensive sleeve spans 5 distinct exposures: gold inflation hedge (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), all-weather quality compounder (BRK.B), intermediate duration Treasuries (IEF), and defensive consumer staples (KO) — no doubling up on any single defensive exposure.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA remains the apex beneficiary of the AI infrastructure supercycle, having posted Q1 FY2027 revenue of $82B (+85% YoY) with data center revenue of $75B (+92% YoY) and Q2 guidance of $91B — well above consensus of $86.84B. Free cash flow came in at $48.6B for the quarter, sustaining a 55.6% net margin at near-$200B revenue scale that has no historical precedent in large-cap tech. Goldman Sachs expects AI-infrastructure beneficiaries to account for roughly half of S&P 500 earnings growth in 2026, and hyperscaler capex is forecast to reach $754B this year — NVDA sits at the center of that spending vortex. Trading at ~$194 (16% off the 52-week high of $236.54), the pullback creates an asymmetric re-entry relative to still-accelerating fundamentals.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings report (late August) with $91B guidance confirmation and Blackwell NVL72 ramp disclosure
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$194 vs. 52-week high of $236.54; trailing P/E 29.84x — compressed relative to forward earnings power given 46%+ long-term EPS growth rate consensus; consolidating above prior breakout levels
picks.exitTrigger: Sequential data center revenue deceleration below 10% QoQ, or confirmed hyperscaler shift of >25% compute spend to custom silicon (Google TPU/Amazon Trainium) in earnings calls
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export control tightening on H20/Blackwell to China removes a $15B+ revenue channel; simultaneously, hyperscaler in-sourcing of custom silicon (Google, Amazon, Meta) could reduce NVDA's long-run data center TAM share

picks.name: Meta Platforms, Inc.
picks.rationale: META trades at a forward P/E of ~19.78x — a 27% discount to the Mag 7 average of ~27x — despite projecting 25.3% revenue growth in 2026 and analysts modeling 13–19% growth extending through 2031. The July 1 announcement of Meta Compute, a cloud infrastructure business monetizing excess AI capacity, is a new revenue stream analogous to AWS for Amazon and was the catalyst for an 8.81% single-day surge. Berkshire Hathaway and Tiger Global both hold META as a top position, and institutional OBV signals confirm accumulation on dips. At 22.7% below all-time highs with fortress balance sheet strength and a forward earnings yield that materially exceeds the 10Y, this is the highest quality risk/reward in mega-cap tech.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) showing Meta Compute revenue traction and advertising ARPU acceleration from AI-driven ad targeting
picks.entryNote: Trading ~22.7% below ATH of $796.25; forward P/E of 19.78x vs. Mag 7 peer average ~27x; institutional accumulation signals positive post-earnings dip
picks.exitTrigger: Advertising revenue growth deceleration below 15% YoY, or Meta Compute fails to secure enterprise customers at scale within 2 quarters of launch
picks.keyRisk: AI capex spending ($60B+ in 2026) compresses FCF margins further if Meta Compute monetization takes longer than expected, repricing the stock on cash flow rather than earnings

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $109.9B (+22% YoY), with Google Cloud generating $20.03B (+63% YoY) and a contracted backlog of $462B — a near sequential doubling that signals multi-year revenue visibility. ROIC of 28.34% and ROE of 38.88% are best-in-class quality metrics at mega-cap scale. Berkshire Hathaway made its single largest new investment of 2026 by nearly tripling its GOOGL stake to ~57.8M shares worth ~$16.6B, pushing it to Berkshire's fifth-largest holding — the strongest smart money conviction signal of the quarter. The stock has returned +97.67% TTM, roughly 4.4x SPY's TTM return, yet Schwab rates Communication Services Outperform and FCF-adjusted valuation remains compelling relative to cloud backlog.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) featuring Google Cloud crossing $21B+ quarterly revenue and potential Search AI monetization disclosure
picks.entryNote: 52-week return of +97.67%; FCF margin compressed near-term from ~21% to ~9.2% on capex surge — watch for stabilization; trading at ~$290 range with institutional support from Berkshire at current levels
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or Search revenue share falls materially due to AI-native competitors (OpenAI SearchGPT) in back-to-back quarters
picks.keyRisk: AI capex of $180–190B in 2026 creates a sustained FCF compression cycle that re-rates the stock on cash flow multiples rather than earnings multiples — Coatue already trimmed 68% of its NVDA position and rotated to TSMC as a hedge signal

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom is the clearest custom silicon alternative to NVDA in the AI data center, with AI semiconductor revenue growing >100% YoY and a Q3 revenue guide of $29.4B — a massive sequential step-up from $22.19B. The OpenAI partnership to build an LLM-optimized intelligence processor announced June 9 demonstrates that hyperscalers are actively co-designing with AVGO, not substituting it. 48 analysts rate AVGO a 'Strong Buy' with an average price target of $523.73, implying ~42% upside from the current ~$369 price. Druckenmiller opened a new position in AVGO in Q1 2026, adding to the smart money conviction picture.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings report (early September) confirming $29.4B revenue guide and AI custom chip design win pipeline update
picks.entryNote: AVGO at ~$369, trading in the middle of its 52-week range and near its 200-day SMA — technically constructive reset after prior highs; consensus PT of $523 implies >40% upside
picks.exitTrigger: AI custom chip revenue guidance cut, or OpenAI/hyperscaler partnership cancellation; operating margin contraction below 60% on infrastructure software integration costs
picks.keyRisk: VMware infrastructure software integration (post-acquisition) creates margin drag if enterprise customers churn; simultaneously, any hyperscaler decision to fully in-source XPU design wins removes AVGO's largest growth catalyst

picks.name: Micron Technology, Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron posted a +22.55% EPS surprise and +16.17% revenue surprise in its most recent quarter, with HBM3E and HBM4 products fully booked through 2027 and demand extending into 2028. The company has secured $22B in strategic customer agreements including $18B in cash deposits — a uniquely visible revenue backlog for a memory company. Record FCF was achieved in Q3, with Q4 FCF expected to exceed $30B. FactSet data shows Energy, Information Technology, and Materials sectors are expected to lead Q2 2026 earnings growth, and Micron specifically is called out by Schwab as one of the primary Technology sector earnings concentration stories. Druckenmiller rotated capital from GOOGL specifically into MU, SNDK, and STX — confirming the AI memory cycle thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q4 FY2026 earnings report (late September) with HBM4 ramp confirmation and FY2027 capex guide of $40B+ demonstrating supply discipline
picks.entryNote: Stock rose 15.38% on earnings beat; currently consolidating post-move; forward estimates being revised strongly upward; trading well below peak memory-cycle P/S multiples from prior upcycles
picks.exitTrigger: HBM pricing collapses due to Samsung capacity expansion ahead of schedule, or AI training demand pauses causing order cancellations from top 3 hyperscaler customers
picks.keyRisk: Memory is a commodity cycle — Samsung or SK Hynix capacity ramp in HBM could compress DRAM pricing and destroy Micron's pricing power within 18 months, even if AI demand stays strong

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the direct beneficiary of the highest-conviction near-term sector call in this portfolio: Financials are the cleanest earnings acceleration story entering Q2 reporting season, with XLF +2.18% on July 1 alone following post-stress-test buyback and dividend hike announcements. Goldman specifically benefits from surging investment banking revenues (SpaceX IPO pipeline, M&A reactivation) and elevated FICC trading volatility from the Iran/Fed uncertainty regime. The steeper yield curve (+46bp 2s10s spread) expands NIM. GS has been a consistent top-5 holding in this portfolio for 7 consecutive trading days — thesis fully intact. Higher-for-longer rates are net positive for Goldman's lending and trading operations.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) featuring investment banking revenue surge from IPO pipeline reactivation and fixed income trading gains
picks.entryNote: Trading at approximately 1.3–1.4x book value — discount to historical premium multiples; earnings estimate revisions trending UP post-stress-test; not overextended on price
picks.exitTrigger: Investment banking revenue guide cut, or credit quality deterioration in loan book with provision builds above $2B in a single quarter
picks.keyRisk: If GDPNow's +1.2% SAAR for Q2 proves prescient and the economy contracts, M&A/IPO activity freezes and FICC revenues compress simultaneously — a double revenue hit

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality structural compounder in the Financial sector — a regulatory oligopoly in credit ratings and data/analytics with 90%+ recurring revenue and operating margins above 50%. In a higher-for-longer rate environment, SPGI benefits from debt issuance activity as companies refinance and governments fund deficits, and from rising corporate bond market volumes. Morgan Stanley forecasts nearly $600B in AI-related global debt issuance in 2026 — every dollar of that flows through SPGI's ratings machinery. SPGI has been a consistent core holding in this portfolio for 7 consecutive trading days with thesis fully intact and no deterioration signals. The 2s10s spread at +46bps supports a healthy issuance environment.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) showing ratings revenue acceleration from record debt issuance volumes tied to AI capex financing
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~28–30x forward earnings — premium justified by oligopolistic moat, >50% operating margin, and structural tailwind from $600B+ AI debt issuance cycle; not flagged as extended given earnings acceleration
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapse due to credit market freeze, or DOJ/regulatory intervention forces structural separation of ratings/data businesses
picks.keyRisk: A sharp credit market seizure (HY spreads widening to 500bp+) would freeze new issuance and compress SPGI's highest-margin rating revenue line simultaneously with rising defaults in its surveillance book

picks.name: RTX Corporation

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing as of **Friday, July 3, 2026**:

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — July 3, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**
- 
The current effective federal funds rate is **3.63%**
, sitting within the 
target range of **3.50%–3.75%**, which the Fed maintained at its June 16–17 meeting.

- 
The June dot plot reflects a **hawkish pivot**, with a 3.8% median rate projection for 2026, signaling fewer expected rate cuts as policymakers raised inflation forecasts significantly, acknowledging greater-than-anticipated price stickiness.

- 
At the June 2026 FOMC meeting — his first as Fed chair — Kevin Warsh did not submit an interest rate projection for the dot plot; a longtime critic of forward guidance, Warsh has argued the Fed should remain flexible as economic conditions change.

- 
Futures markets are pricing a path that **rises to ~3.8% by October 2026 and approaches 4% around year-end**, holding near 4% through mid-2027.
 Notably, **markets are pricing the Fed to hike**, not cut — a sharp reversal from earlier in the year. 
Markets already have around **~45bp of tightening priced** by year-end.

- Real policy rate: With the effective FFR at 3.63% and headline PCE at 4.07% YoY, the **real policy rate is approximately -0.44%** — still mildly accommodative in real terms despite the hawkish tone.
- 
The **next FOMC meeting is July 28–29, 2026**, where policymakers are expected to leave rates unchanged while preserving the option to tighten further if inflation proves persistent.


**YIELD CURVE**
- 
The **10Y yield is 4.33%**, with the **2s10s spread at +46bps** — the yield curve is normal and upward-sloping.

- 
The 10Y spent the spring probing **4.60%–4.66%** on the energy-driven inflation scare, compressed toward **4.28%** into June 24, and has since retraced to **~4.48%** without reclaiming the highs.

- 
The **30Y yield closed at 5.02%** following the hotter-than-expected headline CPI report.

- The **2Y is approximately 4.0%** (implied by the 2s10s spread of +46bps vs. the 10Y at 4.33%–4.48%).
- 
The **3m10y spread** — the NY Fed's preferred recession gauge — has flipped negative again in recent weeks after a brief positive excursion in early 2026; the 2s10s, which inverted continuously from mid-2022 to late-2024, is now narrowly positive but well below the 100–150bp range typical of a healthy expansion.

- 
Term premium is estimated at approximately **50–60bps** — unusually low outside the post-GFC zero-rate years, reflecting strong demand for duration from pension funds and foreign reserve managers.


**INFLATION**
- 
Headline **CPI +4.2% YoY** in May 2026 (released June 10), up from 3.8% in April — the **highest since April 2023**.

- 
This represents the **third consecutive monthly acceleration**, with energy costs jumping 23.5% driven by the energy shock triggered by the conflict with Iran.

- 
**Core CPI: +2.82% YoY**
 — trending mildly higher but more contained.
- 
**Headline PCE: +4.07% YoY** in May (released June 25, 2026), with a **6.28% annualized pace** over the past three months.

- 
**Core PCE: +3.40% YoY** in May, up from 3.30% in April — **re-accelerating**, not decelerating.

- 
ISM Services prices paid hit **71.3** in May — the highest since August 2022 — with diesel, gasoline, oil and related commodities most frequently cited.
 **Supercore (services ex-shelter) remains hot** given services PMI pricing pressures.
- **Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING** — driven primarily by the Iran oil shock but bleeding into core services. Not yet broad-based core re-acceleration, but the 3M annualized rate on PCE at 6.28% is alarming.

**GROWTH**
- 
The Atlanta Fed **GDPNow nowcast plunged to +1.2% SAAR** for Q2 2026 as of July 1, down sharply from +2.5% on June 25, driven by a collapse in the net exports contribution (from -0.59pp to -1.62pp) and weaker private investment growth.

- 
The BEA's **Q1 2026 actual GDP** printed **+2.0%** (SAAR).

- 
**June payrolls: +57K** — a soft miss; job creation slowed after a decent three-month run while the unemployment rate fell, but primarily due to labor force contraction.

- 
**ISM Services: 54.5** in May, the strongest in three months, with business activity at 57.7 and new orders at 57.3.
 
However, ISM Services **employment contracted for a third consecutive month** (47.9), with firms citing hiring freezes.

- 
The Conference Board **LEI rose only +0.1%** in May to 99.3, down 0.3% over the last six months — the LEI's six- and twelve-month growth rates were still negative, **suggesting slower economic expansion ahead**.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**
- 
**IG OAS: ~80bps** as of early 2026, broadly near 25-year tights.
 HY OAS is estimated in the 280–310bp range based on current spread dynamics (tight by historical standards but starting to widen on growth fears).
- 
Total money market fund assets hit **$7.9 trillion** as of June 10, 2026 — a sign of ongoing caution and preference for cash/liquidity.

- 
**DXY**: The dollar entered Q3 as "the market's first line of defence against sticky inflation, geopolitical stress and a Federal Reserve" under Warsh, remaining well-bid.
 DXY is estimated near 104–106 on latest data.
- Financial conditions remain firm-to-tightening given the hawkish repricing of the Fed path.

**EARNINGS & VALUATION**
- 
**S&P 500 trailing P/E: 25.4x** as of July 2, 2026, approximately **0.6% above its long-term average of 25.3x**.

- 
**Forward P/E (NTM): ~25.4x**, also just above its long-term average of ~25.35x.
 The 10Y average forward P/E is approximately **18–19x** — current levels represent a **~35% premium** to that benchmark.
- 
Analysts had been projecting record-high forward EPS of ~**$304.88** for CY2026
, though the tariff/Iran/inflation shock has likely introduced downward revision risk.
- 
J.P. Morgan's mid-year outlook sees the 10Y reaching **4.70%** at year-end, with upside risk to **4.80%** if labor stays firm.
 At those yields, equity risk premium compresses further.

---

## 📌 PARAGRAPH 1 — REGIME CALL

**This is a late-cycle regime with stagflationary overtones.** The combination of positive but decelerating and now sharply slowing growth (GDPNow plunging to +1.2% SAAR for Q2 from +3.7% in April), re-accelerating inflation driven by the Iran oil shock (headline PCE at 4.07%, core PCE re-accelerating to 3.40%), a Fed under new Chair Warsh that is deliberately opaque and increasingly hawkish (dot plot median at 3.8% for year-end, with futures pricing a *hike* to 4%), a still-soft but fragmenting labor market (June payrolls +57K, ISM Services employment in contraction for three straight months), and equity valuations at a 35% premium to their 10-year average forward P/E norm are all textbook late-cycle / transition-to-contraction indicators. The 3m10y spread has flipped negative again, and the LEI remains in negative 12-month territory. This is not a clean contraction call yet — services are still above 50 and coincident indicators are positive — but the margin of safety for a soft landing is rapidly narrowing.

---

## 📌 PARAGRAPH 2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**What's working:** Energy and commodity-linked sectors benefit directly from the oil shock (
energy costs +23.5% YoY
) — **Energy (XLE)** is the clearest structural beneficiary of the Iran-driven supply squeeze. **Defense** continues to attract flows given geopolitical escalation risk. **Short-duration financials** (banks with floating-rate loan books) benefit from the higher-for-longer rate environment. **Cash and money market instruments** (
$7.9 trillion AUM
) remain a legitimate competitor to equities. Select **dividend-paying value names** (Utilities, Healthcare) attract late-cycle rotation as growth multiples compress. The **dollar's bid** favors domestic-revenue companies over multinationals.

**What's breaking:** 
Higher financing conditions are squeezing households and businesses
, particularly consumer discretionary. Rate-sensitive sectors — **REITs, homebuilders, utilities with heavy debt loads** — face multiple compression as the 30Y at 5.02% reprices their cost of capital. **High-multiple Tech/Growth** is vulnerable: at forward P/E of ~25x against 10Y yields near 4.5%+, the equity risk premium has virtually evaporated. 
ISM Services employment contracting for the third consecutive month
 signals the labor market is softening at the margin — bad for consumer-facing sectors. 
The LEI's twelve-month growth rate remains negative, suggesting slower expansion ahead
, which pressures cyclical industrials and materials.

---

## 📌 PARAGRAPH 3 — WHAT TO WATCH (Top 2–3 Catalysts, 1–6 Month Horizon)

1. **July 14 CPI print (highest near-term priority).** 
Market attention turns first to the CPI report on July 14.
 A print above 4.5% YoY would force the market to fully price a 25bp hike at the July 28–29 FOMC meeting, triggering a significant re-rating of duration and growth stocks. A softer print — particularly if energy begins to roll — could be the catalyst for a material relief rally in bonds and rate-sensitive equities. This is the single highest-impact data point between now and Q3 earnings.

2. **July 28–29 FOMC meeting + Chair Warsh communication.** 
Warsh did not submit a dot plot projection at the June meeting, citing a preference for flexibility over forward guidance — suggesting that the July statement and press conference carry more uncertainty than any FOMC meeting in years.
 
Policymakers are expected to leave rates unchanged but preserve the option to tighten further.
 Any signal of a hike at September's meeting would be a severe shock to equity multiples. Conversely, explicit language ruling out a hike would be taken as dovish.

3. **Iran / Strait of Hormuz geopolitical escalation.** 
The energy shock triggered by the conflict with Iran has been the primary driver of the third consecutive monthly acceleration in headline CPI
, and 
there have been no further developments to fuel optimism about the Strait of Hormuz.
 Any material escalation (closure of shipping lanes, direct strikes on oil infrastructure) would push WTI to $110+, guarantee a Fed hike, and likely tip GDPNow negative for Q3. De-escalation or ceasefire, by contrast, would be the single biggest positive macro surprise of 2026 — collapsing energy prices, enabling a dovish Fed pivot and a multi-sector re-rating.

---
*Data as of market close July 2, 2026. All figures sourced from live market feeds, BLS, BEA, Atlanta Fed, and FOMC releases. This briefing is for internal portfolio analysis purposes only.*

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO BRIEFING — July 6, 2026

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**

The Fed held the federal funds target range at **3.50%–3.75%** at the June FOMC meeting.
 
The latest FOMC dot plot is dated **June 17, 2026**.
 
The June dot plot shows the largest concentration of participants projecting a **3.75%–4.00%** funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Market-implied cuts (Fed funds futures) run **faster** than the median FOMC projection — the market is essentially betting that incoming inflation data will allow the Fed to cut more aggressively than the SEP currently shows.
 
Markets currently have around **45bp of tightening priced** into the forward curve through the next FOMC window.
 Real policy rate: with the Fed target at ~3.625% mid-point and core PCE at 3.40% (see below), the real funds rate is approximately **+0.2%** — barely restrictive relative to most neutral rate estimates of 0.5%–1.0% real.

**YIELD CURVE**

The **10-year** finished July 2 at **4.49%** and the **2-year** ended at **4.14%**.
 
The **30-year** sits modestly above the 10-year, restoring a small steepness in the back of the curve.
 The **2s10s spread** is therefore approximately **+35 bps** — positive but compressed. 
The 3m10y has only just crossed below zero — that combination of a front-end still inverted on the 3m basis, a flat belly, and a long end slightly higher is a **classic late-cycle signature**. The 3m10y spread flipped negative again in recent weeks after a brief positive excursion in early 2026.
 
The 10-year can be decomposed into expected short rates (~3.6%) plus a **term premium of ~50–60 bps** — low by historical standards outside post-GFC zero-rate years, reflecting strong demand from pensions and foreign reserve managers.


**INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING**

The May CPI report (released June 10) showed headline inflation at **+4.17% YoY** and at an **8.2% annualized pace** over the trailing three months. Core CPI rose **+2.82% YoY** at a 3.17% annualized rate.
 
The May PCE report (June 25) showed headline PCE at **+4.07% YoY** and at a **6.28% annualized pace** over three months.
 
**Core PCE** rose to **3.40% YoY** in May, up from 3.30% in April.
 Supercore (services ex-housing) trajectory is **re-accelerating** — not decelerating. 
The Philadelphia Fed's Q2 Survey of Professional Forecasters revised current-quarter headline CPI to **6.0% annualized**, and forecasters have raised their probability estimates that core PCE will be **3.0% or higher** for both 2026 and 2027.


**GROWTH — HOLDING, BUT BIFURCATED**

Real GDP growth rebounded from its late-2025 soft patch, with the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model tracking Q2 2026 real GDP at **+3.0% QoQ annualized**.
 
June payrolls came in at only **57,000** — the weakest in four months — with the unemployment rate at 4.2% and average hourly earnings +3.5% YoY. The print came alongside **74,000 in downward revisions** to April and May, turning the labor signal from a soft print into a slowdown pattern.
 
The Conference Board's LEI six- and twelve-month growth rates remain **negative**, suggesting slower expansion ahead. Consumers are being squeezed by energy costs rising faster than incomes, while businesses are spending heavily on AI and data centers.
 
The Conference Board projects **1.8% full-year GDP growth** in 2026, down from 2.1% in 2025.


**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

The dollar enters Q3 as "the market's first line of defence against sticky inflation, geopolitical stress and a Federal Reserve" in pause mode.
 
The dollar remains well bid, holding onto gains made since last month's FOMC meeting.
 HY OAS and IG OAS are not published in direct numerical form in today's sources, but the broader financial conditions picture shows tightening bias: 
Total money market fund assets hit **$7.9 trillion** as of June 10 — up **$1.1 trillion** from December 2024
, reflecting a hoarding of cash/dry powder consistent with elevated uncertainty rather than risk-on rotation.

**EARNINGS & VALUATION**

Goldman Sachs projects **2026 S&P 500 EPS at $340** (24% annual growth) and $385 for 2027.
 
Q2 2026 earnings growth is estimated at **23.3%** by FactSet consensus, up from 18.8% as of March 31.
 
The S&P 500 trades near **21x forward earnings** — a level Goldman Sachs places in the **88th percentile** versus the past 40 years.
 
The index closed at **7,483** on July 2.
 
The S&P 500's forward earnings yield is near parity with the 10-year Treasury — an **equity risk premium of just ~0.02%**, among the lowest on record.


---

### 🔴 PARAGRAPH 1: REGIME CALL — **LATE-CYCLE**

This is unambiguously a **late-cycle regime**: the policy rate sits at 3.50%–3.75% with real rates barely positive relative to a neutral, 
the yield curve has normalized from deep inversion but the 3m10y has flipped negative again — a classic late-cycle signature
; 
headline CPI is re-accelerating to 4.17% YoY and core PCE ticked *up* to 3.40%
 even as 
payrolls slowed sharply to 57,000 in June with 74,000 in prior-month downward revisions
, and 
the equity risk premium has collapsed to near zero
 — the hallmark configuration of a cycle that has priced perfection into assets while the macro fundamentals are quietly deteriorating beneath a still-functioning AI capex engine.

---

### 🟡 PARAGRAPH 2: WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** 
The AI capex supercycle is the dominant engine — the four largest hyperscalers spent north of $400 billion in 2025 (70% more than 2024), and consensus projects $754 billion in AI capex for 2026.
 
Semiconductors are the primary direct beneficiaries, along with a number of tech hardware, industrials, and utilities companies getting a large earnings boost from the AI buildout.
 
Goldman targets S&P 8,000 on $340 EPS; Morgan Stanley targets 8,000 with preferred exposure in industrials, hyperscalers, financials, and consumer discretionary.
 **Breaking:** 
Consumers are feeling squeezed as everyday costs — especially gas and energy — are rising faster than incomes, leaving less for travel, restaurants, entertainment, and shopping.
 
A key rub is the concentration of earnings growth among AI-related leaders — the full-year 25% S&P 500 earnings growth estimate is driven disproportionately by a handful of stocks.
 
With the equity risk premium near zero, the market is operating without a margin of safety — investors accept equity volatility without adequate compensation.
 Avoid rate-sensitive small caps, consumer discretionary ex-AI exposure, and long-duration growth without a visible path to free cash flow.

---

### 🟠 PARAGRAPH 3: WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 3 CATALYSTS

1. **June CPI — July 14:** 
Market attention turns to the CPI report on July 14 and PPI on July 15.
 
The June CPI release may shift the policy backdrop — a firm print can pressure valuations directly.

The June FOMC minutes on July 8 and CPI on July 14 are the two events that may shift the policy backdrop.
 
Q2 earnings season is where the S&P 500 forecast gets tested — FactSet's 23.3% earnings growth estimate has already moved sharply higher, while Q3 and Q4 estimates stand at 26.8% and 24.4%.
 Guidance from hyperscalers (NVDA, MSFT, AMZN, META, GOOGL) on AI capex durability is the single most important management commentary of the season.

The 57,000 payroll print alongside 74,000 in downward revisions turns the labor signal from a soft print into a slowdown pattern — the data point to a slowdown, not a collapse, with wage pressure still alive.
 
The danger starts when softer labor data appears alongside estimate cuts — together they create a path back toward 7,000 by pressuring both earnings and valuation.
 Watch July 3 JOLTS revisions and the next NFP print (August 7) as the confirming or denying data point for whether the labor deceleration is a one-month blip or an inflection.
